metadata.title: A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents

abstract: In the past decades, marine accidents brought the serious loss of life and property and environmental contamination. With the accumulation of marine accident data, especially accident investigation reports, compared with subjective reasoning based on expert experience, data-driven methods for analysis and accident prevention are more comprehensive and objective. This paper aims to develop a content-aware corpus-based model for the analysis of marine accidents to mine the accident semantic features. The general research framework is established to combine accident data, expert prior knowledge, and semi-automated natural language processing (NLP) technology. The NLP models are optimized, fused, and applied to the case study of ship collision accidents. The results show that the proposed model can accurately and quickly extract hazards, accident causes, and scenarios from the accident reports, and perform semantic analysis for the latent relationships between them to extend the accident causation theory. This study can provide a powerful and innovative analysis tool for marine accidents for maritime traffic safety management departments and relevant research institutions.

Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: The paramount importance of maritime transport safety has gradually become a worldwide consensus since the international shipping industry is responsible for the carriage of around 90 % of world trade(K. Liu et al., 2021). According to the "Safety and Shipping Review 2022" by Allianz Global Corporate & Specialty, although the annual shipping losses have substantially declined by 57 % over the past decade, the severe marine accident risk heightens continually due to the Covid-19 pandemic, war conflict, climate change and the increasing of the vessel size (Allianz, 2022). A marine accident would not only cause serious loss of life and property but also may lead to environmental contamination (Mou et al., 2019). For instance, the capsizing of the Eastern Star ship led to 442 people casualties in 2015, and the collision between the Sanchi tanker and CR Crystal freighter caused the largescale leakage of condensate oil and bunker fuel in 2018.

To effectively reduce the probability and severity of maritime accidents, the International Maritime Organization (IMO) and the government of various countries have released or amended many standards, laws, and regulations to strengthen the safety management of maritime transport (Kim, 2020). For example, the Casualty Investigation Code was released by IMO in 2008(IMO, 2008) and the Chinese Traffic Safety Law was released by China government in 2021 (State Council of the PRC, 2021). These policies guide maritime traffic safety management departments and research institutions at all levels to improve accident analysis and investigation methods.

As the Chinese proverb "the overturned cart ahead is a warning to the carts behind" says, marine accident data, such as accident investigation reports, can be used as an important basis for cause analysis of accidents. The accident investigation report is a concluding document formed by the investigation team after completing a series of work, such as on-site investigation, data collection, event description, loss assessment, cause analysis, and liability judgment of the accident. Because accident investigation reports are mainly professional and unstructured text, researchers are often required to have a strong professional background and identify key information manually.

The mechanism of a maritime accident is like a treasure buried in an accident report waiting to be excavated. Obviously, the larger the accident report dataset, the more accurate and clear the mechanism will be. However, increasing human and time costs also make analysis based on accident report datasets more difficult or even impossible to operate. To solve this problem, we need to find an analysis method suitable for the set of marine accident investigation reports based on system thinking and build a systematic model which is both professional and efficient.

sections.paper_id: Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: In the past decades, accident analysis methods are evolving constantly. Different from the past mainstream accident analysis approaches focusing on human factors and linear causal reasoning (Underwood & Waterson, 2014) such as the domino model (Heinrich, 1941), the Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990(Reason, , 2000(Reason, , 2016)), and the Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS) (Wiegmann & Shappell, 2001), the current accident analysis approaches advocate taking system theory as the framework such as the Accident Mapping Analysis (AcciMap) (Rasmussen, 1997), the Formal Safety Assessment (FSA) (IMO, 2015) and the Systems Theoretic Accident Model and Process (STAMP) (Leveson, 2004). More traditional methods, such as Fault Tree (FTA), Event Tree (ETA), and Bow-tie diagram, are more integrated into the above framework as an auxiliary analysis tool. For instance, Zhang et al. (2019) used the fusion method of the FTA and HFACS to analyze the collision risk of an icebreaker on the Arctic route. In addition, the Bayesian Network (BN) (Hänninen, 2014) is popularizing recently in the research of quantitative accident risk analysis, becoming a great implement to the above models for improving their accuracy. For example, Kezhong Liu et al. (2021) developed a datadriven Bayesian Network (BN) to analyze the major accident records in Chinese coastal waters.

Most of the abovementioned approaches need to identify the hazards and accident causes by carefully reading and learning the accident investigation report manually (Cheng et al., 2020), as the foundation of the followed analysis procedures (Zhong et al., 2020). As the IMO and maritime safety administrations of countries around the world pay more attention to the accumulation of historical data and the standardization of accident investigation reports, the scale of accident data set is enlarging, and the text content is also more abundant. In recent years, with the rapid development of NLP technology, to obtain valuable information from an accident report set, keyword extraction and text classification methods have been commonly used. These methods extract the key information from accident data to identify and classify the hazards by using word segmentation and term frequency statistics (Hughes et al., 2018). Common NLP techniques, such as TF-IDF (Jing et al., 2002), latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003), and text-rank (Mihalcea & Tarau, 2004), have been used in the analysis of accident investigation reports. The LDA, as a topic clustering algorithm based on machine learning, can not only extract keywords but also realize text classification by training the topic model. Bomi song uses the LDA algorithm to analyze patient information in the major safety fields to obtain the development trend of safety technology (Song & Suh, 2019).

Although these methods are less laborious and time-consuming, it is difficult to ensure identification accuracy. As one of the state-of-art models in the NLP field in 2018, the BERT model (Devlin et al., 2019), has been rapidly popularized and applied to text mining in various fields (Yao et al., 2019). The BERT is a two-way transformer model based on word embedding and a Deep Neural Network (DNN) (Feng & Chen, 2021), which can learn the relevance between "word-word" and "sentence-sentence" by constructing a vector space of the words. As a pretraining model, the BERT can train the word embedding model suitable for the target corpus in advance and be applied to downstream tasks after fine-tuning, which can significantly improve algorithm performance and reduce the cost of training (Xie et al., 2020). In addition, several recent studies adopted semi-automated NLP techniques. For instance, Hughes et al. (Hughes et al., 2018) considered features of railway accident reports and combined automated keyword extraction software and manual analysis with a bow-tie diagram to improve the accuracy of hazard identification and classification.

Recently, more and more studies combine the BERT with other traditional NLP models for text semantic analysis. These studies rely on BERT's excellent ability for content awareness on the special corpus and further improve the analysis performance by using traditional models such as the conditional random field (CRF) (Blei et al., 2004) and LDA in fine-tuning. However, in the field of maritime traffic safety, most studies only use NLP technology as a tool to extract the key information from accident reports, and rarely analyze the characteristics of accidents at the semantic level.

sections.paper_id: Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: To provide an effective approach to alleviating the workloads and time cost of marine accident analysis, and a new perspective to explore the deep semantic relationship of the hazards and causes in marine accidents, this study is inspired to design and develop a content-aware corpus-based analysis model for marine accidents (CAM-MA). The following research not only identifies and analyzes the hazards and accident causes from the accident data but also conducts a semantic analysis of the marine accidents and gains the semantic relevance of the hazards, causes, and accident scenarios.

To reach the goal, a total of 207 investigation reports of ship collision accidents published by the Maritime safety administration of China are collected. Then the BERT model which is fine-tuning with NER, CRF, and expert prior knowledge is used to extract the hazards and accident causes from the corpus. An LDA topic model is trained to classify the accident scenarios, and the coupling degree and importance designed as semantic indexes are calculated to reveal the deep hidden relationships between the hazards and causes. Moreover, the perplexity and accuracy of the CAM-MA are validated by its efficiency compared with manual identification and classification of the hazards and accident causes. Finally, the prevention suggestions for ship collision accidents are given out in an example accident scenario.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the definitions and the general research framework. Section 3 presents accident data and prior knowledge. Section 4 illustrates the development process of the CAM-MA. Section 5 includes the model train and application performance, accident analysis results, and accident prevention suggestions. Section 6 discusses the model innovation, practical implication, limitation, and improvement potential of the study. Section 7 concludes this research.

sections.paper_id: Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: Before constructing a marine accident analysis framework, it is necessary to define some basic concepts, including maritime transport system, marine accident, accident cause, accident scenario, and hazard.

A maritime transport system (MTS) is a complex and dynamic system composed of the activities of humans and ships with management or organizational constraints in a physical environment (Mullai & Paulsson, 2011). Safety is an eternal theme in the design and operation procedure of the MTS.

Marine accidents are the emergences from the multitudinous unsafe states of the MTS. As same to the term "marine casualty" according to the definitions of the IMO in this paper (IMO, 2008), a marine accident denotes an event, or a sequence of events, that has resulted in any of the following which has occurred directly in connection with the operations of a ship:
1. the death of, or serious injury to, a person;

2. the loss of a person from a ship; 3. the loss, presumed loss, or abandonment of a ship; 4. material damage to a ship; 5. the stranding or disabling of a ship, or the involvement of a ship in a collision; 6. material damage to marine infrastructure external to a ship, that could seriously endanger the safety of the ship, another ship, or an individual; or.
7. severe damage to the environment, or the potential for severe damage to the environment, brought about by the damage of a ship or ships.

An accident cause (AC) means actions, omissions, events, or conditions that lead to an accident (IMO, 2008). The causes of marine accidents include mechanical(ship), human, environmental, and organizational factors(management).
An accident scenario (AS), in this paper, means a set of accident causes that lead to the accident and can be expressed by:

The term "hazard" has many definitions in the field of safety science. It usually refers to the source of danger to a person, property, and environment. Based on the system theory (Leveson, n.d.), in this paper, a hazard denotes a component entity of the MTS or an intrinsic attribute of the component entity. The hazards are interrelated and interact with each other, making the maritime transport system complex and dynamically changing (Puisa et al., 2018). Hazards are closely related to the accident's causes. For instance, negligent lookout, which is the most common unsafe behavior of crews, is an accident cause in this paper, and the crew responsible for the lookout during ship navigation is a hazard related to the cause.

Before constructing a marine accident analysis framework, it is necessary to define some basic concepts, including maritime transport system, marine accident, accident cause, accident scenario, and hazard.

A maritime transport system (MTS) is a complex and dynamic system composed of the activities of humans and ships with management or organizational constraints in a physical environment (Mullai & Paulsson, 2011). Safety is an eternal theme in the design and operation procedure of the MTS.

Marine accidents are the emergences from the multitudinous unsafe states of the MTS. As same to the term "marine casualty" according to the definitions of the IMO in this paper (IMO, 2008), a marine accident denotes an event, or a sequence of events, that has resulted in any of the following which has occurred directly in connection with the operations of a ship:

1. the death of, or serious injury to, a person;

2. the loss of a person from a ship; 3. the loss, presumed loss, or abandonment of a ship; 4. material damage to a ship; 5. the stranding or disabling of a ship, or the involvement of a ship in a collision; 6. material damage to marine infrastructure external to a ship, that could seriously endanger the safety of the ship, another ship, or an individual; or.

7. severe damage to the environment, or the potential for severe damage to the environment, brought about by the damage of a ship or ships.

An accident cause (AC) means actions, omissions, events, or conditions that lead to an accident (IMO, 2008). The causes of marine accidents include mechanical(ship), human, environmental, and organizational factors(management).

The term "hazard" has many definitions in the field of safety science. It usually refers to the source of danger to a person, property, and environment. Based on the system theory (Leveson, n.d.), in this paper, a hazard denotes a component entity of the MTS or an intrinsic attribute of the component entity. paragraphs: The research framework used in this paper consists of four layers: data gathering, prior knowledge, model development, and model application and analysis, as shown in Fig. 1. This study collects the marine accident investigation reports to construct the marine accident corpus (MAC), which is used as a basic dataset for model training. As the analysis and research of marine accidents belong to the subdivided professional field, semantic features of accident causes and hazards are highly professional. If the general NLP model is directly used for the MAC, it is difficult to obtain the expected results. Therefore, based on K. Yan et al. expert experience and literature research, this paper constructs the initial list of accident causes and hazards as prior knowledge (Xu et al., 2021). To analyze the marine accident in the semantic dimension, a content-aware corpus-based analysis model for marine accidents (CAM-MA) is developed with five functional modules including extraction, classification, coupling analysis, importance analysis, and scenario analysis. After model training and application on the MAC, the hazard and accident cause set with the coupling, importance, and scenario analysis results are obtained. Accordingly, visual means such as the chord diagram, scatter diagram, and Sankey diagram, are comprehensively used to visualize the results (Xie et al., 2020). Finally, suggestions on hazard control and accident prevention are summarized.

sections.paper_id: Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: The accident investigation report represents essential material for studying marine accidents. According to the Casualty Analysis Procedure issued by IMO (IMO, 2010), the marine accident or casualty investigation reports are categories based on "initial event": Collision, Stranding or grounding, Contact, Fire or explosion, Hull failure or failure of watertight doors, ports, etc., Machinery damage, Damages to ship or equipment, Capsizing or listing, Missing, Accidents with life-saving appliances, and Other. In China, there were 1,302 marine accidents from 2013 to 2018, including approximately 440 collision accidents, accounting for about one-third (33.8 %) of the overall accidents. Therefore, 207 investigation reports of ship collision accidents from the official website of the China Maritime Safety Administration(China MSA) are used as basic data. The accident investigation reports were compiled following "The Guidelines for the Compilation of Water Traffic Accident Investigation Report." (China MSA, 2016). The core chapters of the accident reports are the accident overview, ship information, accident description, cause analysis, responsibility identification, and management suggestions. The ship types mainly include passenger ships, cargo ships, containers, and fishing ships. More than half of the collision accidents involved fishing vessels.

The number of words in the accident reports ranges from 1,226 Chinese characters to 15,387 Chinese characters. The accident cause is generally classified into four types: man, ship, environment, and management, as a result of the implementation of "The Guidelines for the Preparation of Water Traffic Accident Investigation Report.". To analyze accident causes and hazards, the textual content of sections: "accident cause analysis," "accident responsibility identification," and "safety management suggestions" in each report, are picked to form the MAC.

To achieve more accurate and effective experimental results, the MAC is built into one article set (AS) containing 207 articles and one sentence set (SS) containing 11,589 sentences according to the different granularity. All data of both AS and SS are divided into a training set and a test set at a ratio of 9:1.

paragraphs: The structure of the CAM-MA model is shown in Fig. 2. Five functional modules including extraction, classification, coupling analysis, importance analysis, and scenario analysis are connected.
The specific steps of the CAM-MA model development process are as follows:
(1) The pre-training of the keyword extraction model is based on BERT-CRF, and a priori knowledge is used in the training process.

The accident cause and hazard set obtained in this step denote the basic data of steps ( 2)-( 5); (2) By calculating the coupling degree based on Cosine similarity (Iqbal et al., 2021) between accident causes and hazards, their semantic coupling relationship is obtained and displayed by Chord diagram; (3) By determining the term frequency and coupling connection number of accident causes and hazards, the distributions of accident causes and hazards are displayed in the form of a scattering diagram; (4) In the word segmentation process, the prior knowledge list of accident causes is introduced to enlarge the term frequency. On this basis, the accident scenario classification model is trained based on the LDA topic model (Iqbal et al., 2021). ( 5) Then the coupling degree between the topic words (accident causes) and the hazards is calculated to analyze their semantic coupling relationship in each accident scenario, which is finally displayed in the Sankey diagram.

paragraphs: The pre-trained BERT model uses the bi-directional transformer mechanism of multi-head self-attention, which enables each word in a sentence to be directly encoded with any word in the sentence regardless of the direction or distance, and each word can fuse the information of the adjacent words (Devlin et al., 2019). In addition, in the pre-training process, the BERT model applied the mask language model to cover up a certain proportion of words randomly to improve the effectiveness of the model training. The main method is to select 15 % of the words in the corpus for masking, of which 80 % are replaced by mask, 10 % are replaced by substitute words, and the remaining 10 % remain unchanged.

paragraphs: This paper uses an improved LDA topic model algorithm to classify accident scenarios (Suh, 2021). The accident scenario set is obtained by training the topic model based on MAC, including topics and topic words; meanwhile, each topic represents a unique accident scenario, and each topic word denotes a unique accident cause of an accident scenario. The main steps of developing the scenario classification model are as follows.

Step 1: Text preprocessing. It is necessary to preprocess the corpus; save 187 accident reports as 187 consecutive text segments and then remove irrelevant contents, such as punctuation marks, spaces, and line breaks;

Step 2: Parameter setting. Define a set of parameters for a custom LDA topic model training, mainly including the term frequency amplification coefficient, number of training topics, and number of topic word displays;

Step 3: Set keywords and stop words. Keywords refer to words or phrases that need to be specially divided because the term frequency of accident causes is not the highest in the accident report. To obtain a more accurate model, the term frequency of these words should be amplified. At the same time, some stop words with a high frequency but little significance are removed, such as "accident", "collision", and "safety";
Step 4: Term frequency amplification. Amplify the term frequency of a cause of an accident by:
where TF * k indicates the term frequency of an updated word k; TF k represents the original term frequency; e denotes a coefficient of amplification; ACS represents the accident cause set.

When a word belongs to the ACS, the term frequency increases e times; otherwise, it does not change. The value of e also affects the calculation results of perplexity. The optimal value of e is determined through the perplexity experiment;
Step 5: Model training. The corpus is trained using the LDA topic model to obtain the topic model;

Step 6: Perplexity calculation. The perplexity defines the accuracy of the topic model for topic classification (Blei et al., 2010). The lower the perplexity, the higher the discrimination of the topic model would be, and the better the classification effect is achieved. In this paper, the lower the perplexity is, the clearer the accident scenario classification is. The perplexity is calculated by

where D denotes the test set of a corpus, which contains M documents; N d represents the number of words in a document d, and p(w) represents the probability of the generation of the word w in the document d.
Step 7: Classify and name the scenarios.

paragraphs: The hardware environment used for the training of the extraction model was Quadro RTX 6000 (arch = 7.5); the training size was set to 6,141; the training set was divided into 192 batches for training according to the batch size of 32; the number of epochs was set to four; the Viterbi algorithm and a cross-entropy function are applied to evaluate the accuracy of the model. The results show that the accuracy of the extraction of the model without fine-tuning was 85.95 %; After adding the CRF layer and fine-tuning based on prior expert knowledge, the accuracy of the extraction model was increased to 98.19 %.

paragraphs: The perplexity calculation of an LDA topic model is an effective method for the evaluation of the model performance (Blei et al., 2010). To obtain a more accurate classification model, the perplexity calculation experiment was performed at e = (1/10/100/500/1000/2000), and the maximum number of topics was set to 60. The results showed that the value of e affected the upper limit of the perplexity, and the perplexity decreased significantly with the increase in e at the beginning. When e exceeded the value of 500, the decrease in the perplexity slowed down. In addition, the downward trend of perplexity with the number of topics was unchanged. When the number of topics was 40, the perplexity decreased slowly and even began to increase. When the number of topics and e was too large, an overfitting phenomenon could easily appear. Therefore, finally, the number of topics was set to 40, and e was set to 500 to train the classification model (Fig. 4).

After training, a contrastive analysis based on a test set was applied in the classification model and manual classification of accident scenarios, to evaluate the classification performance. As shown in Table 2, all the 20 reports in the test set are classified by the model separately, and the topic with the highest distribution probability was regarded as the predicted accident scenario. Compared with the results of manual classification, the predicting accuracy of the classification model was 75 %.

According to the term frequency, the top 10 accident causes with the highest term frequency are determined, and they are shown in Table 3.

paragraphs: This paper analyzes the importance of accident causes and hazards based on the scatter diagram. In Fig. 7, the horizontal axis represents the term frequency, and the vertical axis represents the number of coupling connections. The closer the point is to the upper right corner of the scatter diagram, the higher its importance is. Among the hazards, the captain (784, 98) and COLREGS (1242, 54) had the highest importance, and there was a large between them and the other hazards. It is clear that as the highest leader in the process of ship navigation, the captain's decision could often directly affect the risk of ship navigation. The COLREGS is an international general navigation collision avoidance rule. Failing to abide by this rule and take collision avoidance actions could be a direct cause of a ship collision accident. Therefore, COLREGS also had a high degree of importance. In addition, although shipping companies have a high term frequency, their number of coupling connections was not high, which indicated that most shipping companies had only secondary responsibility in ship collision accidents, even if they could have a certain lack of management. As a potential hazard to people, navigation skills play a key role in the safety guarantee of various professional work in the process of ship navigation; thus, they had the highest number of coupling connections. Among the causes of accidents in Fig. 8, poor visibility was the most important factor, but this was not reflected in the importance of hazards. This was mainly because this study regarded the change in visibility as a result of the effect of environmental hazards, such as heavy rain, fog, morning and dusk shadow, and night lights on lookouts during navigation. Therefore, the importance scattered these hazards are not particularly prominent.

The description of the accident investigation report generally emphasizes visibility, but sometimes some of the reasons affecting the visibility are not explained in depth. In addition to poor visibility, negligent lookout and failure to maintain formal lookout are the most important factors, which could also be combined into "lookout error," which was the direct cause of almost all collision accidents. In addition, the causes of accidents such as misoperation and a failure to track with radar also had a similar degree of importance to the skills of people in hazards.

Based on the importance analysis of accident causes and hazards, in the scenario of a collision accident, human factors accounted for a high proportion. Thus, it is necessary to prevent key roles, such as those of captain and second mate, from becoming high-risk hazards; also, the training of human navigation skills and navigation rules is of high importance. In addition, during navigation, especially under the condition of poor visibility, keeping a regular lookout and complying with the collision avoidance rules can effectively prevent the risk of collision.

paragraphs: In this paper, 187 accident reports are used to train the extended LDA topic model which includes 40 scenarios. Each accident scenario has a unique probability distribution of the topic words as shown in Table A.3 (show only the top 5 topic words of each scenario). The higher ratio of the probability decreasing from the topic word #1 to #5 of a scenario, the clearer topic of the scenario. This means that the accident causes in this accident scenario are more concentrated. For instance, the probability decreasing ratio in accident scenario #36 is 90 %, while its top 3 topic words (accident causes) are "Poor visibility"(22.85 %) "Negligent lookout" (10.20 %) "Failure to equip and maintain VHF radio" (3.35 %). We can infer easily the main accident cause of scenario #36 are the bridge crews' performance of negligent lookout when the visibility is poor. However, in scenario #11 (probability decreasing ratio = 9 %), the probability of the top 3 topic words of "No valid certificate of competency" (9.83 %) "Lack of navigational knowledge and skills"(9.68 %) "Failed to perceive in time"(9.24 %) are nearly equal, which means that it is difficult to distinguish primary and secondary causes in scenario #11 and this accident scenario is potentially formed by multiple event chains. We have described all 40 scenarios by combining the top 3 topic words and classified them into 4 categories and 14 subcategories based on expert experience as shown in Table 6.

The coupling and importance of the hazards are analyzed in 5.3.1 and 5.3.2. In this section, the coupling analysis of the hazards and causes is deduced to explore the latent connection between two hazards in different accident scenarios. For instance, in scenario #36, the cause of the "poor visibility" links with the two hazards of "Pilot" and the "Attention" respectively (Fig. 9), helping build a latent link between the two hazards that do not link to each other in Fig. 5. This has shown that in the case of poor visibility, the attention and awareness of the pilot are very important to avoid a collision. Thus, even when the coupling degree between the two hazards did not reach the preset threshold, an indirect relationship could be established in a specific scenario. This indicated that a semantic network between hazards was not only affected by the semantic coupling degree in the word tensor space but also by the accident scenario (Table 7).

paragraphs: Based on the corpus data of the accident investigation report, through coupling, importance, and scenario analyses, the hazards and accident causes with high importance, as well as the potential relationship between them, are identified. To prevent accidents, the hazards should be controlled first (Kwesi-Buor et al., 2019). Hazards with higher importance often have a more important role in accidents. This study puts forward the preliminary prevention and control suggestions for hazards with high importance.

The control strategy for a single hazard is relatively simple and supports further subdivision of the prevention and control strategy to achieve the effects of accurate prevention and control. However, the occurrence of accidents is usually the result of the joint action of multiple hazards. The uncertainty of hazards and their mutual interaction make the accident-cause relationship extremely complex (Fowler & Sørgård, 2000). By constructing an accident scenario set and analyzing the relationship between accident causes and hazards in an accident scenario, this paper puts forward suggestions on accident prevention strategies for different scenarios. Take accident scenario #36 as an example. The main accidents in this scenario are caused by poor visibility, negligent lookout, a failure to equip or effectively use the VHF equipment for communication, and a failure to execute the captain's night navigation command. Poor visibility belongs to the environmental factors, while the latter three factors belong to the human factors. According to the Sankey diagram, there are 62 high coupling hazards related to this scenario, and manship environmental management hazards are 13, 19, 12, and 18. According to the connection relationships and types of hazards, the prevention and control measures, as well as accident investigation suggestions, for this scenario are put forward for two stages, namely before and after the engagement, as shown in Table 8.

sections.paper_id: Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: In this paper, the general research framework and methodology for marine accident analysis are established. From the perspective of the system, after the fusion and enhancement of the NLP models including BIOES, BERT, CRF, and LDA, we build a new accident analysis model: CAM-MA. The results show that the application of the model in the marine accident dataset helps us extract and identify more hazards and accident causes than identification results by an expert group. Moreover, the extraction accuracy of hazards or accident causes and the classification accuracy of accident scenarios in the accident report set reach 98.19 % and 75 % respectively as shown in section 5.1, which also evidence the excellent performance of the CAM-MA.

In addition, CAM-MA has a new extension in the breadth and depth of semantic feature analysis for marine accident report sets. On the one hand, CAM-MA can combine term frequency and cosine similarity to analyze the importance and relevance of hazards or accident causes in maritime accidents, which is more comprehensive and reasonable than semantic feature analysis based only on term frequency. On the other hand, CAM-MA is also based on the optimization LDA and faces different accident scenarios to mine deeper links between hazards, and to find hidden accident evolution chains and risk propagation mechanisms. Table 7 Hazards with high importance. The control measures are the same as those of the "captain".

sections.paper_id: Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: Compared to the current accident analysis model such as the HFACS, AcciMap, and STAMP, CAM-MA as an automated model has advantages in hazard and cause extraction of large-scale data sets, which can save a lot of labor and time cost. Besides, the analysis results of the coupling degree and importance degree of the hazard and accident cause obtained based on the semantic analysis function of the CAM-MA model are consistent with that of the abovementioned approaches, however, with lower subjectivity and uncertainty, which is mainly attributed to the fact that the CAM-MA is a data-driven model. Therefore, the construction of CAM-MA can provide a new and convenient analysis tool for the MSA, which contributes to improving the efficiency and scientificity of maritime traffic accident analysis. Furthermore, it provides a new way of thinking for maritime traffic accident analysis and safety risk assessment.

The improving space for the CAM-MA is also wide-open. First, the accuracy of the model can still be improved with the expansion of the dataset. With the accumulation and sharing of historical maritime accident data worldwide, this improvement is predictable. Second, it is another major improvement focus that accident investigation reports in different languages can be analyzed by the CAM-MA indiscriminately. There have been many successful cases of mixed language processing in other fields, such as academic paper analysis and epidemic case report analysis. Last but not least, the greatest application potential and scenario of the CAM-MA should be combined with other analysis models. For example, the STAMP model needs to identify the control relationship of system functional components based on manual experience. Based on the semantic relationship identification by the data-driven CAM-MA, more control relationships between components can be found in a larger system accident dataset, which can greatly improve the universality and standardization of the STAMP model.

sections.paper_id: Yan et al. - 2023 - A content-aware corpus-based model for analysis of marine accidents
paragraphs: In this paper, focusing on the hazards and accident causes of the ship collision accident, a content-aware corpus-based model for the analysis of marine accidents is developed by a fusion of advanced NLP techniques and prior knowledge based on expert experience. The model applied in the marine accident corpus consists of 207 investigation reports of ship collision accidents from the official website of the China MSA, extracts 132 hazards and 139 accident causes, and categorizes the ship collision accident into 40 scenarios. The semantic analysis methods including importance analysis, coupling analysis, and scenario analysis are utilized to identify the hazards and accident causes with the most contribution to the accident, and dig their latent inside connection in different accident scenarios. According to the analysis results, the prevention recommendations for the ship collision accident are formed.

As an automated content analysis model, the CAM-MA can accurately and quickly extract the key information in the accident report and perform comprehensive and scientific accident analysis, which is expected to provide a powerful tool for maritime traffic safety management departments and relevant research institutions, thus greatly reducing the labor and time costs in hazard identification. Furthermore, the latent relationship of hazards, causes, and scenarios mined by the CAM-MA is a significant discovery to extend the accident causation theory, which can increase the depth of the analysis and be used to construct a more sophisticated risk knowledge map and risk spread networks towards marine accidents in the future.
